evidence.text: Average number of full-time equivalent employees increased by 4.7% to 12,739.

factors.summary: No significant impact from trade tariffs mentioned in the provided text.

evidence.text: No specific mention of trade tariffs in the provided text.

factors.summary: R&D investment is focused on in-flight entertainment and aircraft upgrades.

evidence.text: Launched Alaska Beyond™ in-flight experience featuring gourmet Tom Douglas signature meals, new streaming in-flight entertainment, and power at every seat on our 737-800/900/900ER aircraft.

factors.summary: Digital automation is being implemented through new in-flight entertainment systems and online services.

evidence.text: Launched online self-tag baggage options for passengers flying from Seattle to San Diego, Anchorage, and Juneau. Launched Alaska Beyond™ in-flight experience featuring new streaming in-flight entertainment, and power at every seat on our 737-800/900/900ER aircraft.

factors.summary: New products include in-flight entertainment upgrades and new customer service initiatives.

evidence.text: Launched Alaska Beyond™ in-flight experience featuring gourmet Tom Douglas signature meals, new streaming in-flight entertainment, and power at every seat on our 737-800/900/900ER aircraft. Launched online self-tag baggage options for passengers flying from Seattle to San Diego, Anchorage, and Juneau.

factors.summary: Competitive dynamics are intense, with Delta increasing capacity and other airlines entering new markets.

evidence.text: Currently, we see strong demand for 2015, and because of our strong balance sheet and the structure of our fleet, we will flex our fleet to meet demand and allocate capacity in the markets that meet our return objectives. This includes our expectation to grow departures out of Seattle by 10% in 2015. Additionally, competitive capacity is expected to be 15% higher in the first quarter and 12% higher in 2015 based on current schedules. We expect Delta Air Lines, our largest direct competitor, to increase its overlapping capacity from 41% in 2014 to 50% in 2015.

factors.summary: Differentiation is strong through customer service, brand loyalty, and operational efficiency.

evidence.text: Ranked 'Highest in Customer Satisfaction Among Traditional Network Carriers' by J.D. Power and Associates for the seventh year in a row. Named

factors.summary: The margin trajectory shows mixed performance, with gross margins facing pressure from increased costs and fuel expenses, while operating margins are improving due to cost management and declining fuel costs.

evidence.text: Gross margins are under pressure due to increased costs, but operating margins are improving due to cost management and efficiency.
Gross and operating margins are compressing slightly, primarily due to fuel cost changes and competitive pressures.
Adjusted pretax margin of 17.2% in 2014 compared to 12.4% in 2013. Economic fuel cost per gallon decreased by 6.7% to $3.08.

factors.summary: The company is actively implementing cost reduction measures, including efficiency programs and aircraft improvements, which have resulted in measurable savings, particularly in fuel efficiency and operational efficiency. These actions are part of a broader strategy to manage expenses, preserve liquidity, and return capital to shareholders through stock repurchases and dividends.

evidence.text: We continued to return capital to our shareholders by repurchasing $348 million of our common stock in 2014, and paid dividends totaling $68 million. [...] We will continue to focus on preserving a strong liquidity position and evaluate our cash needs as conditions change.

Lowered unit costs excluding fuel and special items for the fifth consecutive year, to the lowest level ever. Added split-scimitar winglets to 48 planned aircraft, which are expected to improve fuel efficiency by 1.5% per aircraft.
evidence.subsection: Low Fares, Low Costs, and Network Growth

factors.summary: The company's backlog orders are growing, supported by firm orders and options for additional aircraft, as well as new deliveries and contracts. This growth aligns with business expansion and increased operational needs, as evidenced by the increase in full-time equivalents to support the expanding operations. However, there is no direct evidence suggesting any challenges or negative impacts related to the backlog orders in the provided subsections.

evidence.text: At December 31, 2014, we had firm orders to purchase 76 aircraft. In addition, we had options to acquire 48 additional B737 aircraft and options to acquire five Q400 aircraft.
The increase in FTEs is to support the growth in our business.

Delivered ten additional Boeing 737-900ERs, which will further strengthen Alaska's fuel-efficient fleet. Exercised options for two Q400 aircraft to be delivered in 2017, and ordered an additional Q400.
evidence.subsection: Low Fares, Low Costs, and Network Growth

factors.summary: Fuel costs are a significant portion of operating expenses, and the company is exposed to volatility in jet fuel prices.

evidence.text: All of our future oil positions are call options, which are designed to effectively cap the cost of the crude oil component of our jet fuel purchases. [...] With call options, we are hedged against volatile crude oil price increases.

Fuel costs constitute a significant portion of our total operating expenses, accounting for 32% , 34% and 35% of total operating expenses for the years ended 2014 , 2013 and 2012 , respectively. Future increases in the price of jet fuel may harm our business, financial condition and results of operations, unless we are able to increase fares and fees, or add additional ancillary fees to attempt to recover increasing fuel costs.
evidence.subsection: Our business, financial condition, and results of operations are substantially exposed to the volatility of jet fuel prices. Significant increases in jet fuel costs would harm our business.

evidence.text: The significant decline in fuel prices over the past several months improves our financial outlook as fuel is one of our largest operating expenses. Economic fuel cost per gallon decreased by 6.7% to $3.08.

factors.summary: The company is improving its debt leverage, as evidenced by a lower debt-to-capital ratio and active share repurchase programs, which contribute to a stronger balance sheet and higher credit ratings.

evidence.text: Because of our strong balance sheet and financial performance, in 2014 we became one of only two airlines in the U.S. with investment grade credit ratings. [...] Long-term debt-to-capital ratio [...] decreased from 35%:65% to 31%:69%.

Lowered adjusted debt-to-total capitalization ratio to 31% as of December 31, 2014. Repurchased 7,316,731 shares of common stock for an average price of $47.23 during 2014 for $348 million , or 6.9% of market capitalization at the beginning of 2014 .

factors.summary: The company has consistently returned capital to shareholders through a combination of stock repurchases and dividend increases, demonstrating management's confidence in the company's future performance. The evidence highlights significant repurchases and a notable increase in dividend payments, which are positive indicators for shareholder value.

evidence.text: The company repurchased $348 million of its common stock in 2014 and paid dividends totaling $68 million.

Declared a $0.20 per share dividend, up 60% from the prior quarter. Repurchased 7,316,731 shares of common stock for an average price of $47.23 during 2014 for $348 million, or 6.9% of market capitalization at the beginning of 2014.

factors.summary: Free cash flow is improving, driven by strong operating cash flows and effective cost management. The company generated over $1 billion in operating cash flows in 2014 compared to $981 million in 2013, and also reported $336 million in free cash flows in 2014.

evidence.text: In 2014, we generated over $1 billion in operating cash flows compared to $981 million in 2013.
Cash provided by operating activities was $981 million in 2013, compared to $753 million in 2012.
Generated over $1.0 billion in operating cash flows and $336 million in free cash flows in 2014.

factors.summary: The company anticipates a significant increase in capital expenditures, driven by aircraft deliveries and infrastructure improvements, which indicates confidence in growth but may strain cash flow.

evidence.text: Our capital expenditures were $694 million in 2014, [...] This is due to the delivery of ten B737-900ERs, the completion of our B737 cabin improvement project, and the exercise of 16 B737 options, two Q400 options, and deposits for an incremental Q400.
We expect depreciation and amortization expense to increase approximately 15% in the next year as we continue to purchase aircraft and make other customer focused investments.

Delivered ten additional Boeing 737-900ERs, which will further strengthen Alaska's fuel-efficient fleet. Exercised options for two Q400 aircraft to be delivered in 2017, and ordered an additional Q400.
evidence.subsection: Low Fares, Low Costs, and Network Growth

factors.summary: The company faces mixed economic influences, with favorable liquidity and strong cash flows supporting operations, but also exposed to potential negative impacts from economic uncertainty and competitive pressures.

evidence.text: Because of our strong balance sheet and financial performance, in 2014 we became one of only two airlines in the U.S. with investment grade credit ratings. [...] We believe that our current cash and marketable securities balance combined with future cash flows from operations and other sources of liquidity will be sufficient to fund our operations for the foreseeable future.

The airline industry is particularly sensitive to changes in economic conditions. Unfavorable U.S. economic conditions have historically driven changes in travel patterns and have resulted in reduced spending for both leisure and business travel.
evidence.subsection: Economic uncertainty or another recession would likely impact demand for our product and could harm our financial condition and results of operations.

evidence.text: Increased competition in the state of Alaska and along the west coast put downward pressures on yield and load factor.

factors.summary: The company's pricing power appears to be mixed. While there is evidence of successful implementation of new fee structures and increases in ticket yields, there are also challenges in raising prices due to increased competition and economic sensitivity, as well as slight decreases in revenue per available seat mile (RASM).

evidence.text: Unfavorable economic conditions also hamper the ability of airlines to raise fares to counteract increased fuel, labor, and other costs.
evidence.subsection: Economic uncertainty or another recession would likely impact demand for our product and could harm our financial condition and results of operations.

evidence.text: The decrease in PRASM was driven by a 0.9% decrease in ticket yield and a 0.5 point decrease in load factor.

The increase in operating expenses was primarily due to increases in wages and benefits, contracted services and depreciation expense as we expanded our fleet and entered into new markets where we almost exclusively use vendors for our station operations.

factors.summary: The company's interest rate impact is mixed. While they have secured variable rate credit facilities, which allow them to manage interest rate changes without immediate borrowing plans, certain financing agreements impose financial covenants that could be negatively affected by interest rate changes, potentially impacting their financial health.

evidence.text: The Company has two $100 million credit facilities. Both facilities have variable interest rates based on LIBOR plus a specified margin. [...] The Company has no immediate plans to borrow using either of these facilities.

Certain of our credit facilities and indentures governing our secured borrowings impose certain operating and financial covenants on us. Such covenants require us to maintain, depending on the particular agreement, minimum liquidity and/or minimum collateral coverage ratios, and other negative covenants customary for such financings. A decline in the value of collateral could result in a situation where we may not be able to maintain the required collateral coverage ratio.
evidence.subsection: Certain of the Company’s financing agreements have covenants that impose operating and financial restrictions on the Company and its subsidiaries.

factors.summary: Volume trends are mixed. While the company has seen growth in unit volumes and demand, there are also stable volume trends in key markets. However, external factors could impact demand, particularly in the Seattle hub which accounts for a significant portion of their flights.

evidence.text: Our strategy is to focus on serving a few key markets... A significant portion of our flights occur to and from our Seattle hub. In 2014, passengers to and from Seattle accounted for 61% of our total passengers.
evidence.subsection: We depend on a few key markets to be successful.

evidence.text: Fuel gallons consumed increased 4.9% in line with the increase in departures and capacity.
Revenue passengers increased by 6.8% to 29,278 thousand, driven by a 6.5% increase in RPMs and a 7.1% increase in ASMs.

factors.summary: The company is experiencing favorable trends with expanding capacity and stable load factors, coupled with increasing revenue per available seat mile. However, there are risks related to external factors that could impact capacity and load factors due to reliance on partner airlines for certain arrangements.

evidence.text: We regularly engage in analysis and discussions regarding our own strategic position, including alliances, codeshare arrangements, interline arrangements, frequent flier program enhancements, and may have future discussions with other airlines regarding similar activities.
evidence.subsection: We rely on partner airlines for codeshare and frequent flier marketing arrangements.

factors.summary: The company faces exposure to environmental regulations which could increase costs, but it is also making efforts towards environmental compliance through investments in fuel-efficient aircraft and sustainability initiatives.

evidence.text: There are a number of legislative and regulatory initiatives and reforms at the federal, state, and local level, including increasingly stringent laws protecting the environment, minimum wage requirements, and health care mandates that could affect our relationship with our workforce and cause our expenses to increase without an ability to pass through these costs.
evidence.subsection: Economic uncertainty or another recession would likely impact demand for our product and could harm our financial condition and results of operations.

evidence.text: Delivered ten additional Boeing 737-900ERs, which will further strengthen Alaska's fuel-efficient fleet. Added split-scimitar winglets to 48 planned aircraft, which are expected to improve fuel efficiency by 1.5% per aircraft.
evidence.subsection: Low Fares, Low Costs, and Network Growth

factors.summary: The company faces potential risks from regulatory changes, particularly in areas requiring significant compliance costs. However, they have successfully addressed accounting standards related to mileage plans, mitigating past issues.

evidence.text: Airlines are subject to extensive regulatory and legal requirements, both domestically and internationally, that involve significant compliance costs. In the last several years, Congress has passed laws, and the U.S. DOT, the TSA and the FAA have issued regulations that have required significant expenditures relating to the maintenance and operation of airlines and establishment of consumer protections.
evidence.subsection: We rely on third-party vendors for certain critical activities.

evidence.text: In 2013, we modified and extended our co-branded credit card agreement with Bank of America Corporation (BAC). In connection with this agreement and as a result of applying related accounting standards, we recorded a one-time, non-cash Special mileage plan revenue item of $192 million primarily related to our revaluation of the deferred revenue liability related to miles previously sold to BAC.

factors.summary: The company faces potential litigation risks, particularly related to aircraft accidents, which could significantly impact its financial results and reputation. However, current litigation exposure appears manageable with no significant pending cases reported.

evidence.text: An accident or incident involving one of our aircraft or an aircraft operated by one of our codeshare partners or CPA carriers could involve a significant loss of life and result in a loss of confidence in our airlines by the flying public and/or aviation authorities. We could experience significant claims from injured passengers, by-standers and surviving relatives, as well as costs for the repair or replacement of a damaged aircraft and its consequential temporary or permanent loss from service.
evidence.subsection: Our reputation and financial results could be harmed in the event of an airline accident or incident.

evidence.text: No significant litigation or investigations mentioned in the provided text.

factors.summary: M&A activity is focused on strategic initiatives rather than acquisitions, with no significant activity reported.

evidence.text: We continue to face strong competition from other carriers due to restructuring, consolidation, and the creation and modification of alliances and joint ventures. Since deregulation, both the U.S. and international airline industries have experienced consolidation through a number of mergers and acquisitions. Carriers may improve their competitive positions through airline alliances, slot swaps/acquisitions, and/or joint ventures.
No significant M&A activity mentioned in the provided text.

factors.summary: Labor costs are a significant and growing concern, creating potential margin pressure. While manageable within the context of overall growth, they are increasing due to higher wages and benefits, which could adversely affect financial performance.

evidence.text: Labor costs are a significant component of our total expenses, accounting for approximately 41% , 42% and 42% of our non-fuel operating expenses in 2014 , 2013 and 2012 , respectively. Each of our represented employee groups has a separate collective bargaining agreement, and could make demands that would increase our operating expenses and adversely affect our financial performance if we agree to them.
Wages increased 6% , primarily due to a ratified contract with Alaska's pilots that was effective April 1, 2013.

The increase in operating expenses was primarily due to increases in wages and benefits, contracted services and depreciation expense as we expanded our fleet and entered into new markets where we almost exclusively use vendors for our station operations.

factors.summary: Labor relations exhibit mixed signals, with some stability indicated by successful contract negotiations and employee programs, yet there are concerns about potential future instability due to union demands and challenges in workforce engagement.

evidence.text: Although we have been successful in maturing communications, negotiating approaches, and other strategies to enhance workforce engagement in the Company's long-term vision, future uncertainty around open contracts could be a distraction, affecting employee focus in our business and diverting management’s attention from other projects and issues.
The increase in FTEs is to support the growth in our business.

Signed a five-year agreement with Alaska Airline's Flight Attendants in December 2014. Signed a six-year contract with Horizon's aircraft technicians and fleet service agents in June 2014. Signed a five-year contract with Alaska's clerical, office, and passenger service employees in April 2014. Signed a four-year contract with Horizon's dispatchers in April 2014.

factors.summary: Overall demand trends indicate a positive outlook with stable or increasing demand, supported by higher passenger traffic and ticket yields.

evidence.text: Increased flying in 2014 as we increased capacity and entered into new markets.

The growth in revenues of $212 million was primarily due to an increase in passenger revenue of $312 million resulting from a 6.5% increase in traffic and 0.7% higher ticket yields, partially offset by a special mileage plan revenue item of $192 million resulting from the adoption of new accounting standards in the prior year.

factors.summary: The company faces challenges in talent retention, particularly in skilled positions, due to competition and potential risks like attrition and pilot shortages. However, they have implemented incentive programs that have successfully awarded significant amounts to employees over the years, which may support retention efforts.

evidence.text: We compete against the major U.S. airlines and other businesses for labor in many highly skilled positions. If we are unable to hire, train and retain qualified employees at a reasonable cost, sustain employee engagement in the Company's strategic vision, or if we are unsuccessful at implementing succession plans for our key staff, we may be unable to grow or sustain our business. In recent years, there have been pilot shortages in the regional market. Attrition beyond normal levels could negatively impact our operating results and our business prospects could be harmed.

Awarded a record $116 million in incentive pay to employees for 2014 , or more than one month's pay for most employees. Over the last five years, employees have earned more than $473 million in incentive pay, averaging 8.7% of annual pay.

factors.summary: Input costs, particularly fuel, are declining, which is expected to positively impact margins. The company is addressing the cost reduction through pricing and productivity improvements.

evidence.text: The raw fuel price per gallon decreased 6.3% as a result of lower West Coast jet fuel prices.
The significant decline in fuel prices over the past several months improves our financial outlook as fuel is one of our largest operating expenses.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is accelerating, driven by factors such as capacity expansion, yield improvements, and increased traffic. Passenger revenue has seen significant increases, contributing to overall revenue growth.

evidence.text: Total operating revenues increased $499 million, or 11%, during 2013 compared to the same period in 2012.

The growth in revenues of $212 million was primarily due to an increase in passenger revenue of $312 million resulting from a 6.5% increase in traffic and 0.7% higher ticket yields, partially offset by a special mileage plan revenue item of $192 million resulting from the adoption of new accounting standards in the prior year.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting towards higher-margin segments, particularly in the Mileage Plan™ program and other net revenues. There is evidence of growth in both the Mileage Plan™ program and other areas such as bag fees and ticket change fees, contributing to an increase in overall other net revenue.

evidence.text: Other – net revenue increased $68 million, or 13%, from 2012. This is primarily due to an increase in our Mileage Plan™ revenues of $47 million or 22%, as a result of a higher rate per mile sold to Bank of America Corporation (BAC) under our new affinity card program and growth in the Mileage Plan™ program.

Other—net revenue increased $91 million, or 16%, from 2013. This is primarily due to an increase in our Mileage Plan™ revenues of $39 million or 15%, due to increase in miles sold and an increase in cash received per mile. Additionally, bag fees and ticket change fees are up 23% and 12%, respectively, due to changes in our fee structure that took effect in November 2013.

factors.summary: Management has provided mixed forward guidance for 2015, with expectations of higher expenses due to increased wages, benefits, and pension costs, while also signaling strong demand and a focus on growth and profitability through capacity expansion.

evidence.text: We expect wages and benefits to be higher in 2015 compared to 2014 on a 3% to 4% increase in FTEs and higher pension expense of approximately $20 million.

Currently, we see strong demand for 2015, and because of our strong balance sheet and the structure of our fleet, we will flex our fleet to meet demand and allocate capacity in the markets that meet our return objectives. This includes our expectation to grow departures out of Seattle by 10% in 2015.

factors.summary: The company faces mixed near-term risks, including fluctuating fuel prices and challenges in maintaining labor agreements, as well as increased competitive pressures and capacity expansions from Delta Air Lines.

evidence.text: The raw fuel price per gallon decreased 6.3% as a result of lower West Coast jet fuel prices.

Expect Delta Air Lines, our largest direct competitor, to increase its overlapping capacity from 41% in 2014 to 50% in 2015. Competitive capacity is expected to be 15% higher in the first quarter and 12% higher in 2015 based on current schedules.

factors.summary: Management exhibits mixed confidence, with cautious optimism in growth areas and strategic investments, yet shows strong confidence through increased dividends, share repurchases, and significant investments in new routes and aircraft.

evidence.text: We expect selling expense to increase in 2015, reflecting our growth in passengers.

Declared a $0.20 per share dividend, up 60% from the prior quarter. The dividend will be paid on March 10, to shareholders of record as of February 24, 2015. Repurchased 7,316,731 shares of common stock for an average price of $47.23 during 2014 for $348 million, or 6.9% of market capitalization at the beginning of 2014.

factors.summary: The company's long-term strategy emphasizes fleet expansion, capacity growth, network growth, cost efficiency, and enhancing customer experience through investments in new aircraft, infrastructure, and customer-focused initiatives.

evidence.text: We expect depreciation and amortization expense to increase approximately 15% in the next year as we continue to purchase aircraft and make other customer focused investments.

Long-term, we plan to vigorously defend our markets through great customer service, Mileage Plan™ promotions, schedule changes, community events, and additional advertising efforts. We will also continue to focus on lowering unit costs so that we can compete more effectively.

factors.summary: Fuel prices and labor costs are key sensitivities impacting near-term results, with fuel prices showing significant volatility. While lower fuel prices have improved financial outlooks, competitive capacity from major airlines like Delta Air Lines remains a critical factor affecting results.

evidence.text: The decrease in raw fuel price per gallon during 2014 was due to a decrease in average crude oil prices of 5.2% and decrease in refining margins of 15.7% , as compared to the prior year.

The significant decline in fuel prices over the past several months improves our financial outlook as fuel is one of our largest operating expenses. Expect Delta Air Lines, our largest direct competitor, to increase its overlapping capacity from 41% in 2014 to 50% in 2015.

factors.summary: Capacity utilization is increasing, supported by higher fuel efficiency and increased departures, as well as new routes and fleet expansion.

evidence.text: Fuel efficiency as measured by ASMs per gallon improved by 2.1%. Added split-scimitar winglets to 48 planned aircraft, which are expected to improve fuel efficiency by 1.5% per aircraft.
Increased fuel efficiency (as measured by seat-miles per gallon) by 2.1% over 2013.

factors.summary: The company is actively expanding its fleet with a focus on fuel-efficient aircraft to enhance efficiency and support growth. The strategy includes purchasing new aircraft, exercising options for additional planes, and upgrading existing fleets with new seats and power systems.

evidence.text: We expect depreciation and amortization expense to increase approximately 15% in the next year as we continue to purchase aircraft and make other customer focused investments.

Delivered ten additional Boeing 737-900ERs, which will further strengthen Alaska's fuel-efficient fleet. Exercised options for two Q400 aircraft to be delivered in 2017, and ordered an additional Q400. Added new Recaro seats and power at every seat for 95 aircraft.
